errors_intro.label: Common Errors + Debugging: Read the Message, Fix the Code
errors_intro.summary: Learn to recognize NameError, TypeError, and ValueError, then practice quick debugging patterns and safe conversions.

sketch.title: Common Errors: NameError, TypeError, and Debug Tricks

identify_error.title: Identify the error type (NameError vs TypeError vs ValueError)

fix_type_mismatch.title: Fix a type mismatch (convert and add numbers)

errors.title: Common Errors: NameError, TypeError, and Debug Tricks
errors.bodyMarkdown:
Mistakes are a normal part of programming.

When Python raises an error, it’s giving you **information about what went wrong**.
Learning to read errors is one of the fastest ways to improve.

Here are three common ones beginners encounter.

---

## 1) NameError — using a variable that doesn’t exist

~~~python
print(score)
~~~

If the variable `score` was never created, Python cannot use it.

Common causes:
- a typo: `scroe` instead of `score`
- using a variable before assigning a value

---

## Try it

Run this (it works):

~~~python
score = 10
print(score)
~~~

Now delete the line:

~~~python
score = 10
~~~

Run the code again.
You should see a **NameError** because the variable no longer exists.

---

## 2) TypeError — incompatible types

Sometimes values exist, but they **cannot be combined the way you tried**.

~~~python
age = input("Age: ")   # string
print(age + 1)         # 🚫 string + int
~~~

The variable `age` contains **text**, not a number.

Fix it by converting the type:

~~~python
age = int(input("Age: "))
print(age + 1)
~~~

---

## 3) ValueError — invalid value for a conversion

~~~python
age = int("twelve")  # 🚫 ValueError
~~~

Python is trying to convert text into a number, but the text **does not represent a valid number**.

Valid examples:

~~~python
int("12")
float("3.5")
~~~

Invalid examples:

~~~python
int("twelve")
float("hello")
~~~

---

## A simple debugging technique

When something looks wrong, inspect the value and its type.

### Print the value
~~~python
print("value:", x)
~~~

### Print the type
~~~python
print("type:", type(x))
~~~

This quickly reveals many beginner mistakes.

---

## Quick mental model

- **NameError** → the variable doesn't exist
- **TypeError** → the types don’t work together
- **ValueError** → the value is not in the expected format

Learning to interpret these messages will save you a lot of time while programming.

m1_types_errors_sc.title: Match the error to the problem
m1_types_errors_sc.prompt:
A student runs this code:

~~~python
age = input("Age: ")
print(age + 1)
~~~

What error will Python raise?
m1_types_errors_sc.hint: input() returns a string, and you can’t add a string and an int.

help.concept: TypeError happens when values exist but their types cannot be used together the way the code expects.
help.hint_1: The variable age exists, so this is not NameError.
help.hint_2: The problem is trying to combine a string with an integer.

m1_err_nameerror_sc.title: NameError: label doesn’t exist
m1_err_nameerror_sc.prompt:
What error do you get if you run this?

~~~python
print(score)
~~~

(Assume `score` was never created.)
m1_err_nameerror_sc.hint: NameError happens when you use a variable name that doesn’t exist yet.

help.concept: NameError means Python cannot find the variable name you asked for.
help.hint_1: The variable score was never assigned.

m1_err_typeerror_sc.title: TypeError: types don’t mix
m1_err_typeerror_sc.prompt:
A shopping app stores the item count as a number:

~~~python
count = 3
print("Items: " + count)
~~~

What error will this cause?
m1_err_typeerror_sc.hint: You can’t concatenate a string and an int. Convert with str(count).

help.concept: TypeError happens when Python cannot combine two values because their types are incompatible for that operation.
help.hint_1: Both values exist, so it is not NameError.
help.hint_2: The issue is joining a string and an integer.

m1_err_valueerror_sc.title: ValueError: invalid conversion
m1_err_valueerror_sc.prompt:
A user typed `twelve` for their age.

What happens here?

~~~python
age = int("twelve")
~~~
m1_err_valueerror_sc.hint: ValueError happens when conversion fails because the text isn’t a valid number.

help.concept: ValueError means the type conversion function is fine, but the specific value is not in the expected format.
help.hint_1: int(...) expects text that looks like a whole number.
help.hint_2: The word twelve is not valid numeric text for int().

m1_err_debug_combo_sc.title: Best quick debug combo
m1_err_debug_combo_sc.prompt: When something behaves weirdly, what’s the best quick debug combo for beginners?

options.a: Print the value and print the type
options.c: Delete the file and rewrite everything

m1_err_debug_combo_sc.hint: Value + type solves most beginner confusion fast.

help.concept: Printing both the value and its type quickly reveals many beginner bugs, especially around strings versus numbers.
help.hint_1: One print shows what the data is right now.
help.hint_2: The other print shows what type Python thinks it is.

m1_err_fill_nameerror_meaning.title: What NameError means
m1_err_fill_nameerror_meaning.prompt: Fill in the blank with the best phrase.

help.concept: NameError is about a missing or misspelled variable name.
help.hint_1: Python cannot use a name it cannot find.
help.hint_2: The variable does not exist yet.

m1_err_fill_typeerror_meaning.title: What TypeError means
m1_err_fill_typeerror_meaning.prompt: Complete the sentence with the best word.
m1_err_fill_typeerror_meaning.template: TypeError means the ____ do not work together.
m1_err_fill_typeerror_meaning.hint: Think about strings, ints, and floats.

help.concept: TypeError is about incompatible data types.
help.hint_1: The values may exist, but they cannot be used together in that operation.
help.hint_2: The problem is with the types.

m1_err_fill_valueerror_meaning.title: What ValueError means
m1_err_fill_valueerror_meaning.prompt: Fill in the blank with the best word.
m1_err_fill_valueerror_meaning.template: ValueError means the value is in the wrong ____.
m1_err_fill_valueerror_meaning.hint: Think about converting text to numbers.

help.concept: ValueError happens when the function is correct, but the specific input value is invalid for that conversion.
help.hint_1: This often appears during int(...) or float(...).
help.hint_2: The value has the wrong format.

m1_err_fill_debug_value.title: First debug print
m1_err_fill_debug_value.prompt: Fill in the blank with the best word.
m1_err_fill_debug_value.template: A quick debug step is to print the ____.
m1_err_fill_debug_value.hint: You want to see what is actually stored.

help.concept: Printing the current value helps you verify what data is really stored in a variable.

m1_err_fill_debug_type.title: Second debug print
m1_err_fill_debug_type.prompt: Complete the sentence with the best word.
m1_err_fill_debug_type.template: Another quick debug step is to print the ____.
m1_err_fill_debug_type.hint: This tells you whether Python sees text, int, float, and so on.

help.concept: Printing the type helps you see whether Python is treating a value as text, an integer, a float, or something else.
help.hint_2: Print the type to confirm what Python sees.

m1_err_reorder_debug_steps.title: Order the debug steps
m1_err_reorder_debug_steps.prompt: Reorder the steps in a sensible beginner debugging flow.

help.concept: A strong beginner debugging flow starts with reading the message, then inspecting the program state, then fixing the code.
help.hint_2: Read first, inspect second, then fix.

m1_err_reorder_nameerror_fix.title: Order the NameError fix
m1_err_reorder_nameerror_fix.prompt: Reorder the steps to fix a missing variable problem.
m1_err_reorder_nameerror_fix.hint: The variable must exist before you use it.

help.concept: To fix NameError, define the variable before the line that uses it.

m1_err_reorder_type_fix.title: Order the TypeError fix
m1_err_reorder_type_fix.prompt: Reorder the steps for fixing a string-versus-int mismatch.

help.concept: When input gives text, you must convert it before numeric operations will work.

m1_err_reorder_safe_parse.title: Order the safe parsing flow
m1_err_reorder_safe_parse.prompt: Reorder the steps for avoiding ValueError with simple validation.

help.concept: Validation should happen before a risky conversion, so you only call int(...) when the input looks safe.
help.hint_1: The digit check comes before int(...).
help.hint_2: Print the final result after conversion.

m1_err_reorder_error_models.title: Order the error models
m1_err_reorder_error_models.prompt: Reorder the three quick mental models in the lesson order.
m1_err_reorder_error_models.hint: Follow the lesson’s quick mental model list.

help.concept: The lesson introduces three common beginner errors in a specific order to help you recognize patterns quickly.

m1_err_identify_error_code.title: Identify the error type
m1_err_identify_error_code.prompt:
A beginner runs code that tries to print `score`, but `score` was never created.

Write a program that prints ONLY the name of the error Python would raise.
m1_err_identify_error_code.hint: Think about a missing variable name.

help.concept: This exercise checks whether you can recognize that using a variable that was never created leads to NameError.
help.hint_1: The variable name is missing, not the type conversion.
help.hint_2: Print only the exact error name.

m1_err_fix_type_mismatch_code.title: Fix the type mismatch (add two numbers)
m1_err_fix_type_mismatch_code.prompt:
A calculator app reads two numbers from the user.

Read TWO inputs.
Convert them to integers.
Print ONLY their sum.
m1_err_fix_type_mismatch_code.starterCode:
a = input()
b = input()
# TODO: convert and print sum

help.concept: This checks whether you know that input values start as strings and must be converted before addition works as math.
help.hint_1: Read both values first, then convert them.

m1_err_parse_age_safely_code.title: Avoid ValueError (basic validation)
m1_err_parse_age_safely_code.prompt:
A website asks for age as text.

Read ONE input (a string).

Rules:
- If it looks like a whole number (digits only), convert it to int and print:
  Next year = <age+1>
- Otherwise print:
  Invalid age
m1_err_parse_age_safely_code.hint: Use text.isdigit() to check. Then int(text).

help.concept: This exercise checks whether you can avoid ValueError by validating text before converting it to an integer.
help.hint_1: Only call int(...) when the text contains digits only.
help.hint_2: Print Next year = <value> for valid input, otherwise print Invalid age.
